the_one_thing.statement: In a scaled picture graph, each symbol represents MORE than one item—specifically, each symbol = 2 items, so we count by 2s (not by 1s) to find totals.

critical_misconception.label: Counting Symbols Instead of Scaling
critical_misconception.why_critical: Students ignore the scale value and count pictures directly. When scale says 'each symbol = 2' and there are 5 symbols, student answers '5' instead of '10.'

the_one_thing.success_indicator: Student correctly creates a picture graph where 10 items = 5 symbols AND can explain 'each symbol shows 2.'
the_one_thing.biggest_risk: Students who don't internalize 'symbol ≠ item' will struggle with all subsequent scale work (M3: 1:5, M4: 1:10) and the multiplication bridge in M7.

student_facing_goal: You'll learn to read and create picture graphs where each symbol shows MORE than one item. One picture = 2 items. Let's see how that works—that changes everything!
exit_check_tests: Can student create a scaled picture graph (1:2) from a data table?
Can student determine correct number of symbols for even values (8 items = 4 symbols)?
Can student determine correct number of symbols for odd values (7 items = 3.5 symbols)?
Can student explain that half-symbol represents 1 item ('half of 2 = 1')?
Can student answer 'how many more/fewer' questions using their created graph?
learning_goals: Interpret scaled picture graphs to generate questions about the data.
Draw scaled picture graphs to represent data with scales of 2. Solve one- and two-step 'how many more' and 'how many fewer' problems using scaled picture graphs.

vocabulary_staging.approach: Use naturally during animation; 'Watch—your 10 items become 5 groups of 2'; informal, no formal definitions
Explicitly name after animation; 'This is a SCALED picture graph. The scale is 2. The KEY tells you the scale. Always check the key first.'
Explicitly teach during first odd number encounter; 'Half of 2 is 1. A half-symbol shows 1 item. Odd numbers need half-symbols.'
Expect student use in context; brief reinforcement only
Expect natural student use; consolidate with comparison to 1:1 scale

bridge.from_module_1: Students read picture graphs and bar graphs with 1:1 scale. They understand that 'the KEY shows the SCALE' and practiced 'scale of 1' language. They can solve 'how many more/fewer' problems by subtracting and 'in all' problems by adding. They understand both horizontal and vertical orientations.
bridge.this_module: Students experience the critical leap: each symbol can represent MORE than one item. Through watching collected data transform via Grouping Animation (10 items → 5 groups of 2 → 5 symbols), creating scaled picture graphs (1:2) from data tables, learning half-symbols for odd numbers ('half of 2 = 1'), and applying M1 comparison skills to their created graphs.
bridge.to_module_3: Students will apply their understanding of 'each symbol = multiple items' to scale of 5. The grouping concept (groups of 2 → groups of 5) and half-symbol understanding transfer directly. Skip-counting connection becomes more explicit.

available_visuals.description: Picture Graphs are the primary visual for M2. Used in Mode 2 (Creating) throughout — students place symbols via click-to-set. Half-symbols introduced in Lesson Section 2. Data Collection Game generates even-only values for Warmup. Grouping Animation is the KEY TEACHING TOOL — appears in Warmup (W.3) and Lesson Section 1 (1.3) to visually transform items into scaled symbols. Data Tables used in two display modes: Symbol Display (Sections 1-2) and Numeric Display (Section 3, Exit Check).

symbol_display.description: Value column shows grouped symbols matching scale. Scale indicator visible: 'Each [symbol] = 2.'

visible_in: Lesson Section 1 (Symbol Display — alongside graphs during teaching)
Lesson Section 2 (Symbol Display — alongside creation tasks)
Lesson Section 3 (Numeric Display — students must convert mentally)
Exit Check EC.1 (Numeric Display — reinforces why key matters)
hidden_in: Lesson 1.6 and 1.7 (students must read from graph alone)
Lesson 2.5 and 2.6 (students must apply scale independently)
Exit Check EC.4 (assessment of scaled graph reading)

constraints: 1:2 scale only throughout entire module — each symbol equals 2 items
Picture graph key must always read 'Each [symbol] = 2 [items]'
Grouping Animation must appear in Warmup AND Lesson Section 1 (non-negotiable)
Even values only in Warmup and Lesson Sections 1-2; odd values introduced in Section 2 and used freely in Section 3+
Half-symbols introduced in Lesson Section 2 only — not before
Pre-completion progression: 3 pre-filled → 2 pre-filled → 0 pre-filled across Sections 1-3
Bar graphs are NOT used in M2 — picture graphs only
Symbol Display mode used in Sections 1-2; Numeric Display in Section 3 and Exit Check
Data Collection Game generates 3 categories with even values in 6-14 range
Picture graph categories: 3 in Warmup, 4-5 in Lesson Sections 1-2, 4 in Section 3 and Exit Check
Orientations: Warmup/Section 2 horizontal; Sections 1 and 3 vertical
Scale reflection (1:2 vs 1:1 side-by-side) appears in Interaction 3.2b as observation only — students do NOT create 1:1 graphs

advanced_vocabulary: skip counting (as formal strategy name — informal use okay)
advanced_concepts: Two-step problems as single submissions (M6 deep practice)
Creating graphs with more than 4 categories
required_phrases: The KEY tells you the scale
The key is like a decoder
Scale of 1 means each symbol shows 1 item. Scale of 2 means each symbol shows 2 items.
[Number] is an EVEN number—it groups perfectly into 2s

forbidden_phrases.reason: Casual use okay; formal term is M3

forbidden_phrases.phrase: You're so smart! / Perfect! / Amazing! / Excellent!

misconceptions.description: Students ignore the scale value and count pictures directly. Trigger behavior: when scale says 'each symbol = 2' and there are 5 symbols, student answers '5' instead of '10.' Student counts the pictures, ignoring the key/scale.
why_it_happens: Natural inclination to count what you see
Key/scale may be visually small or overlooked
Abstract concept that picture 'stands for' something other than itself
prevention: Grouping Animation visually shows transformation (items → groups → symbols)
Explicit key attention: 'ALWAYS check the key first. What does each symbol represent?'
Distractors that ARE symbol counts catch the error in interactions 1.6 and 3.4

remediation.light: Check the key. Each symbol = how many?
remediation.medium: Return to grouping animation; show 5 symbols unpacking to 10 items
remediation.heavy: Side-by-side: same 10 items shown as 10 symbols (1:1) vs 5 symbols (1:2)

misconceptions.description: With 'each symbol = 2' and 5 symbols shown, student calculates 5+2=7 instead of 5×2=10. Trigger behavior: student treats the scale value as a number to add rather than a multiplier.
why_it_happens: Addition is the dominant operation in Grades 1-2
'Each symbol = 2' might be interpreted as 'add 2 to the count'
prevention: 'Groups of' language: '5 symbols means 5 groups of 2—that's 2, 4, 6, 8, 10'
Visual unpacking: show each symbol 'opening up' to reveal 2 items
Skip-counting: count by 2s rather than emphasizing any operation
Interaction 1.2 includes 'Add 2 to each number' as explicit distractor

remediation.medium: Animate symbols splitting into items; count all items
remediation.heavy: Physical grouping with manipulatives or interactive grouping tool

misconceptions.description: With scale of 2 and data showing 7, student draws 3 or 4 complete symbols instead of 3.5. Trigger behavior: student may not realize leftover items need representation, or rounds to nearest whole number of symbols.
why_it_happens: Student may not realize 'leftover' items need representation
May round to nearest whole number of symbols
prevention: Dedicated interaction for first odd number (explicit half-symbol teaching)
Mathematical precision: 'Half of 2 = 1. A half-symbol shows 1 item.'
Even/odd prediction: 'Is 7 even or odd? Odd numbers need half-symbols.'
Visual confirmation: half-symbol clearly shows half the image

remediation.light: 7 is 6 plus 1 more. How many symbols for 6? Now add a half for the 1.
remediation.medium: Animate: 7 items → 3 groups of 2 with 1 leftover → 3.5 symbols
remediation.heavy: Side-by-side comparison of 6 (3 symbols) vs 7 (3.5 symbols)

phases.purpose: Activate prior knowledge of picture graphs from M1 while introducing the critical new concept: scaled representation. Students collect personal data, PREDICT how grouping will work, WATCH their prediction confirmed through animation, then VERIFY they can read the result. Creates ownership ('my data'), cognitive engagement ('was I right?'), and confidence before Lesson formalizes vocabulary.

variables.format: Data Collection Game → Prediction → Grouping Animation → Verification + Bridge

interactions: W.1: Data Collection Game — Mario Party-style counting, 3 categories, even values only
W.2: Review + Prediction — confirm counts, ask 'how many groups of 2' (no feedback; animation reveals answer)
W.3: Grouping Animation — items → groups of 2 → symbols for all 3 categories (observation only with prediction stakes)
W.4: Verification + Bridge — read symbol count from completed graph; bridge to Lesson

phases.purpose: Establish that each symbol = 2 items through animation, vocabulary formalization, and guided graph creation. All values are even numbers—students master whole symbols before encountering half-symbols. Covers CRA: Concrete (animation), Relational (notice pattern), Abstract (formalize 'scale of 2'), Application (create graphs).
interactions: 1.1: Your Scaled Graph — bridge from Warmup; introduce term 'scaled picture graph'; no student prompt
1.2: What Is a Key? What Is Scale? — explicit key/scale vocabulary with MC (includes 'Add 2' distractor for Misconception #2)
1.3: Using the Key to Read — new graph; 'What is the scale?' then 'How many voted for [category]?' with symbol-count distractor
1.4: Your Turn to Create (Guided) — first even-number creation; 3 pre-filled, student fills 2
1.5: More Practice with Even Numbers — second even-number creation rep on same graph
1.6: Checking the Graph Step A+B — identify most symbols, then apply scale for actual value (no data table; symbol-count distractor included)
1.7: How Many More? — comparison question on created graph (symbol-subtraction distractor included)

phases.purpose: Introduce half-symbols for odd values. Explicitly teach 'half of 2 = 1' and connect to even/odd understanding. Students practice creating graphs with half-symbols using horizontal orientation.

variables.data_table_mode: Symbol Display → transitions to no table in 2.5-2.6

interactions: 2.1: The Problem with 7 — animation shows odd number grouping with leftover; MC on what to do with remainder
2.2: Half-Symbol Teaching — animation shows half-symbol solution; confirm '3.5 symbols = 7 items' with MC
2.3: Create with Half-Symbols (Guided) — place symbols for odd value; 2 pre-filled categories
2.4: Complete the Graph — place symbols for even value; confirm no half-symbol needed
2.5: How Many In All? — 'in all' question using created graph (no data table)
2.6: Comparison with Odd Values — 'how many fewer' using scaled values from odd categories

phases.purpose: Remove scaffolding. Students create full graphs from numeric tables without animation. Multiple odd numbers require half-symbols. Students answer multi-step comparison questions. Scale reflection (3.2b) builds metarepresentational competence.
interactions: 3.1: Create with Mixed Values — first category only; odd value; student places 4.5 symbols
3.2: Create Full Graph — complete all remaining categories (mix of even and odd)
3.2b: Scale Reflection (Observation Only) — student's 1:2 graph alongside system-generated 1:1 graph; click which uses fewer symbols
3.3: Who Read the Most? — identify category with most symbols/votes
3.4: How Many in All? — two-category total from completed graph
3.5: Two-Step Problem (Chained) — Step 1: add two categories; Step 2: compare sum to third category

phases.purpose: Verify Lesson understanding before Practice. Tests whether student can understand scale meaning, determine correct symbol counts including half-symbols for odd numbers, create scaled picture graphs, and read values from completed graphs.
interactions: EC.1: Scale Meaning (IDENTIFY) — 'What does scale of 2 mean?' MC with misconception distractors; tests Lesson 1.2
EC.2: Symbol Calculation for Odd Number (IDENTIFY) — how many symbols for odd value? MC includes half-symbol option; tests Lessons 2.1-2.2
EC.3: Create with Half-Symbol (CREATE) — place symbols for odd value category; tests Lessons 2.3-2.4
EC.4: Read Total Value (IDENTIFY) — how many items from completed graph? symbol-count distractor included; tests Lessons 1.3, 2.5

phases.purpose: Consolidate understanding that scaled graphs (1:2) use fewer symbols than 1:1 graphs for the same data. Connect scale concept to efficiency. Confirm half-symbol understanding. Reflect on what 'scale' means. This is NOT more practice—it is connection-making and consolidation.

variables.special_interaction: Scale toggle in S.1: student toggles between 'Each symbol = 1' and 'Each symbol = 2'; graph animates symbol count changes

interactions: S.1: Representation Transfer — scale toggle (1:2 ↔ 1:1); graph animates; MC confirms which scale uses fewer symbols
S.2: Pattern Discovery — multi-select which values need half-symbols; follow-up MC on what half-symbol shows
S.3: Metacognitive Reflection — MC on what 'scale' means; consolidates key/scale vocabulary
S.4: Identity-Building Closure — specific growth statement; bridge to M3 ('Next time, scale of 5—you already know how to count by 5s')
